content:
- Pluxee Check-in with Ramki - Monthly Office Hours
- Digital Twin Structured Summary – April 7, 2025

II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call:

- This Pluxee monthly check-in, led by Ramki Gaddipati, was structured around aligning studio and engineering teams on transition plans and critical build initiatives for the Pluxee India/Philippines program. The session began with detailed introductions and an emphasis on transition support for new team members. The agenda then progressed through in-depth updates on codebase understanding (with a push for a “code jam” approach), major ongoing initiatives in mobile engineering and performance observability, necessary process improvements in regression testing and automation, and clarity on performance monitoring via dashboards. Ramki reiterated the importance of standardizing KPIs and productivity metrics and was particularly focused on team accountability and collective ownership, guiding the group through open QA/UAT blockers and strategies for improvement, all while managing real-time client escalations and planning for an upcoming high-visibility workshop.

IV. Key Takeaways from the Call:

- Team Transition and Onboarding Emphasis: Ramki reaffirmed his personal support and resource availability for all new joiners, expecting smooth knowledge transfer and accountability without added pressure ("Count me as a resource...that's the commitment I wanna make").
- Code Universal Ownership Mandate: Ramki set a non-negotiable outcome for all developers to achieve holistic understanding of the codebase via a mandatory “code jam” (every developer must affirm “I know the code,” not just a part). This is positioned as the first and only milestone before any delivery progress is tracked.
- Mobile Stack Streamlining: Teams are actively working to remove obsolete layers (“door”) in mobile stacks to improve observability, reduce support costs, and facilitate integration, with clear alignment on making necessary endpoint and CA/CSN flow tracking changes.
- Performance and Observability Simplification: Ramki guided the group to focus performance monitoring solely on Pluxee customization and mobile APIs, excluding unrelated systems and enforcing “wiping off” secondary scope to maintain sharp focus.
- Reporting and Metric Standardization: Ramki insisted on adopting story points–based KPI predictability and productivity tracking for the studio, deprecating item-count–based reporting in favor of metrics that reflect true engineering throughput.
- QA/UAT Process Overhaul: In response to repeated QA/UAT slowdowns, Ramki directed a pivot towards daily regression for Tier 1 flows, with emphasis on internal responsibility for test suite setup rather than external dependencies or reactive practices.
- Client Escalation Management: The escalation regarding production testing and UAT breakages was addressed by Ramki steering the team towards enforcing proper test environment/tracking practices, rather than making exceptions for client requests.
- Workshop Representation: On upcoming Pluxee-Tachyon integration workshop, Ramki left team representation flexible but advised circulation for possible adjustment, ensuring suitable Zeta-side technical coverage.
- Staffing and Squad Structuring: Squad formalization into Echo and Titan was acknowledged, with staffing for both customer success and SRE mostly stabilized.
- CAPTCHA/Bot Management: Clarified that CAPTCHA is being adopted for bot management (as opposed to identity management), with tasks distributed across backend/frontend, focusing on specific conversion points.

V. Ramki's Insights:

1. On Team Transition & Support: Ramki emphasized a culture of supportive leadership, promising availability and advocating for a “no added pressure” transition for new program managers, while expecting proactive pushback if his involvement is seen as pressure. He signaled an intent to shield new members from external pressure but stressed team accountability and transparency in challenges.

2. Codebase Clarity & Engineering Discipline: He imposed a clear engineering expectation: all developers must know the entirety of the relevant codebase firsthand. He dismissed outdated, partial knowledge models and previous attempts at process documentation, underscoring “code is truth” and requiring transparent, collective competence as the only milestone to be tracked and reported on.

3. Sharp Focus and Scope Management: Ramki’s leadership approach consistently directed the team to narrow focus, limit reporting and monitoring to essentials, and sharply prune any scope creep. He instructed wiping off non-essential items from tracking—“it is important to say what we won't do”—and demanded teams avoid unnecessary process complexity.

4. KPI/Metric Standardization: Ramki challenged legacy reporting by insisting on activity tracked through story points and delivery predictability, discouraging superficial item count metrics. He stressed the need for a standardized, capacity-reflective reporting system to appreciate engineering efforts and complexity.

5. QA/UAT Accountability: When faced with QA/UAT blockages and escalations, Ramki placed responsibility squarely on the engineering team, insisting on daily regression test operation for Tier 1 flows and dismissing the narrative of external environment reliance. He pushed for self-containment in testing practices and highlighted proactive detection over reactive fixing.

6. Handling Client Escalations/Practices: Ramki demonstrated forthrightness in navigating client pressure—balancing the need to protect the team from unfair demands while also holding the group to engineering best practices. He advised clarity in communicating business criticality, recommended not overcomplicating incident severity realignment, and pointed out the necessity of owning and fixing process gaps internally.

VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items:

Action Item 1
- Task: Run a mandatory “code jam” for all developers, ensuring each can affirm full competence across all necessary repositories relevant to Pluxee scope.
- Assigned To: Entire development team (with Balaji and leads facilitating).
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: No exceptions or partial ownership; every developer must state confidence in understanding the entire code as relevant for their scope. Exclude trivial/unused repositories to stay focused.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: As soon as the fully formed team is in place and before tracking any further progress; urgent first milestone.

Action Item 2
- Task: Limit observability and performance monitoring to Pluxee customization and mobile APIs, and remove (“wipe off”) all secondary dashboards/scope until core areas are fully green.
- Assigned To: Performance/observability/engineering leads (primarily Abdul, Ashish, and associated teams).
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Only cover APIs critical to Pluxee; do not maintain dashboards/monitors on unrelated systems; clearly state and communicate what is excluded from scope.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediate/ongoing; until Pluxee customization and mobile APIs are fully green.

Action Item 3
- Task: Standardize productivity and predictability reporting around story points capacity completed, with historic trend context, replacing item-count–based slides.
- Assigned To: Management/PMO team (Balaji, studio leads).
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Align capacity/delivery tracking to story points, highlighting over-delivery/stretch, and ensure all KPIs are contextualized via engineering metrics.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: From next reporting cycle/meeting.

Action Item 4
- Task: Daily regression automation focused on Tier 1 flows, with priority for P0/P1 scenarios, and proactive test environment management.
- Assigned To: QA team, with support from Arvind and new QA lead.
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Prioritize automated regression for Tier 1 flows; ensure earliest detection of breakages; eliminate dependence on external clusters for core testing.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediate and ongoing; no further delivery milestones until baseline regression is running daily.

Action Item 5
- Task: For the upcoming Pluxee integration workshop, circulate attendee list and allow for dynamic adjustment to representation as per stakeholder guidance.
- Assigned To: Balaji (with Ramki's oversight).
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Share names, let relevant teams (studio, CS, engineering) add/remove as required closer to date.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Before the workshop (scheduled for April 28-29).

VII. Jira Ticket:

- Scope:
- Jira for Code Jam Full Codebase Ownership
- Assignee: Engineering Lead (Balaji), all developers tagged in.
- Justification: This is a top-priority, cross-team initiative requiring tracked participation and affirmation from every developer to prevent silos and guarantee readiness for critical deliverables.

- Scope:
- Jira for Regression Automation (Tier 1 Flows)
- Assignee: QA Lead (Arvind), Automation QA team.
- Justification: Addressing ongoing QA/UAT bottlenecks and client escalations requires a dedicated, time-bound, and trackable automation initiative.

- No Jira Tickets:
- For high-level reporting standardization, observability-scope narrowing, or workshop attendee list coordination, Jira tickets are not appropriate. These are procedural/administrative actions or executive instructions handled outside engineering/delivery work tracking.

VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions:

- Consider establishing a standing, periodic (e.g., bi-weekly) review of codebase “ownership” status until the code jam affirmation is universally achieved; flag and support lagging members.
- Once regression automation for Tier 1 flows is live, institute a dashboard/report that auto-alerts on coverage gaps or missed runs, ensuring sustained discipline.
- Consult with BI and engineering management to define a shared story points allocation model for ease of cross-team predictability reporting, possibly via PM tools integration.
- For future client escalations tied to UAT/process breakdowns, draft a RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) map to clarify ownership for test environments and incident escalation paths.

attendees: Saith Abhi; some may have joined part of the conversation or were relevant stakeholders)
entities: Pluxee transition, team onboarding, code jam, codebase clarity, mobile stack cost optimization, observability, dashboards, performance center, regression automation, QA/UAT, client escalation, staffing, squad structure (Echo/Titan), CAPTCHA/bot management, KPI standardization, workshop planning, Pluxee integration.
